CSCI-2960.description: This course provides an introduction to competitive programming, with a special emphasis on writing code to solve real problems that appear in various programming contests. Students will learn to apply different techniques they learned in CSCI 2300 to write correct and efficient solutions in code in addition to being introduced to new algorithmic techniques and approaches. Course (Student) Learning Outcomes (CLOs): (1) students will gain practical experience in collaborative software development on real-world projects of their choosing; (2) students will learn practical aspects of community driven software development; (3) students will learn and apply the principles of open source on real life projects in an open environment; (4) students will be given the opportunity to learn new technologies of their choosing and will need to develop capabilities to "self learn" new topics areas. This is a third-year programming course. As a result, it assumes a level of academic maturity appropriate for a student in their sophomore or junior year. It also requires good working knowledge of data structures and algorithms, and proficiency in C++ programming or Python.
CSCI-2960.name: Introduction To Competitive Programming

CSCI-4960.description: Machine learning (ML) has demonstrated superior performance in many areas and has increasingly been deployed in real-world critical applications. However, the vulnerabilities and privacy risks of ML models could put public safety and user privacy in danger. Existing studies have shown that the ML applications such as financial analytics and autonomous vehicles are vulnerable to attacks that can manipulate the models to their malicious ends. This course will introduce potential vulnerabilities of ML models, recent research and future directions about the security and privacy problems in real-world machine learning systems. We will cover different types of attacks against computer systems leveraging ML as well as defense techniques, privacy-preserving ML, and robustness and fairness in ML. Students will familiarize themselves with the emerging body of literature on each topic, understand different algorithms, analyze security vulnerabilities, and develop the ability to conduct research projects on related topics.
CSCI-4960.name: Security & Privacy Of Ml

CSCI-4961.description: This course covers network security and defense mechanisms to thwart a variety of attacks. Topics include packet sniffing and spoofing, attacks on the TCP/IP protocol, attacks on the ICMP, ARP protocol, firewall, DNS security: local DNS attack, remote DNS attack, virtual private networks, public key infrastructure, transport layer security, and CDN security.
CSCI-4961.name: Network Security And Defense

CSCI-4962.description: This course will provide an in-depth overview of Cloud Computing and associated virtualization technologies.  Key areas of study will include review of virtualization concepts , cloud service models,  virtual machines (VMs), Docker, portability, and VM migration.  Various virtualization software packages will be leveraged including AWS, Azure, VMWare, VirtualBox, and Docker. This is a hands-on course, and all topics presented will be reinforced by developing solutions using these technologies.
CSCI-4962.name: Cloud Computing

CSCI-4964.description: This course introduces quantum computing through the lens of classic CS computer organization and software engineering. The course is guided by the principle that "you don't really understand something until you can code it"; every concept covered in class will be reinforced by hands-on implementation. Students will not only study the theoretical foundations of quantum computation but will also build software artifacts that translate theory into practice. Participants will learn how to design and implement the abstractions that connect low-level hardware with high-level applications, forming the essential layers of the quantum computer and its associated software stack. Through a combination of lectures, coding projects, and real-world case studies, students will (a) build a solid understanding of quantum computing theory toward more advanced studies, (b) implement foundational data structures for quantum computing, as well as key quantum algorithms in software, (c) explore workload optimization, compilation, error mitigation, result validation and resource management in quantum systems, and (d) gain experience with modern quantum programming frameworks and hardware backends. By the end of the course, students will have developed the technical skills and practical experience necessary to become the workforce powering the next generation of quantum computing, creating tools, frameworks, and platforms that enable application developers, researchers, and domain experts to fully leverage quantum technologies.
CSCI-4964.name: Quantum Computer Organization

CSCI-4965.description: This advanced theory course studies the design of "economic algorithms," such as student-school assignment, auctions, and multi-candidate elections, with an emphasis on improving their simplicity and quantifying their complexity. We will ask: How can we make the algorithms' outcomes transparent and their properties clear? What happens if participants are not perfectly rational, or the designer has only limited information or control? Topics may include market and information design, social choice theory, behavioral economics, interactive mechanisms, and communication complexity. For each topic covered, we will introduce classic results, study recent research papers, and have a problem set focused on theory. Prior experience with mathematical proofs (or significant effort) is required. Final course projects may be theory-focused (e.g., presenting or extending the results of a recent paper) or more-applied (e.g., running simulations, analyzing data, or building an implementation informed by course themes).
CSCI-4965.name: Designing Economic Algorithms

CSCI-4966.description: Haskell is a functional programming language increasingly used in industry; many Haskell features and ideas have been adopted by mainstream languages such as Python. The class will cover basic lambda calculus (the theoretical foundation of functional programing languages) and explore features such as lazy evaluation and infinite structures, higher-order functions, static types, and error reporting. It will cover more advanced Haskell features as well: monads (including lists and IO), type classes, polymorphism, Hindley-Milner type inference and other. Grading will be based on class participation, in-class open-note quizzes, small biweekly programming assignments, and a larger final programming assignment.
CSCI-4966.name: Programming In Haskell

CSCI-4968.description: This course provides an in-depth exploration of operating system concepts and mechanisms beyond the introductory level. Students will study the design, implementation, and evaluation of modern operating systems, with a focus on advanced topics such as system calls, CPU scheduling, concurrency, synchronization, memory management, file systems, virtual memory, and device drivers. The course combines theoretical foundations with practical experience, including programming assignments that involve developing and analyzing operating system components. By the end of the course, students will gain a thorough understanding of the principles and challenges in building robust, efficient, and secure operating systems, preparing them for research or careers in systems software development.
CSCI-4968.name: Advanced Operating Systems

CSCI-6970.description: This course introduces methods for automating the process of decision-making. Starting with the concept of probabilistic reasoning, this course will provide a foundation for a sequential decision-making framework, which can be applied in various domains including autonomous driving, exploration, and robotics. The focus will specifically be on decision making problems under state uncertainty and finally multi-agent systems. Topics include: Bayesian networks, Markov decision process, partially observable Markov decision processes, multi-agent reasoning.
CSCI-6970.name: Decision Making Under Uncertainty
